doc.title: Stock issued for noncash assets
doc.event: Kerr Corp. acquires land by issuing 5,000 shares of its $10-par common stock, which is actively traded at $28 per share. Prepare the entry.

variants.label: Actively traded stock — its market price measures the deal

entries.caption: 5,000 shares of $10-par stock (trading at $28) exchanged for land

lines.label: 5,000 × $28 stock FV
lines.why: Record at the MORE CLEARLY DETERMINABLE fair value. The stock trades daily at $28; the land's value is an appraisal opinion — the market quote wins: 5,000 × $28 = $140,000.
lines.trap: The seller's asking price and the land's tax-assessed value are both noise. Determinability decides, and an active market beats an appraisal.

lines.label: 5,000 × $10 par
lines.why: Par discipline never bends — par × shares to the stock account even in a noncash deal.
lines.trap: Same #1 error as cash issuances: the stock account never takes the full $140,000.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common
lines.label: the excess
lines.why: Deal value minus par-capital, exactly as if the $140,000 had arrived in cash and been spent on land a second later.
lines.trap: No gain, no loss — issuing your own stock is a capital transaction whatever you receive for it.

computationPaths.narration: One measurement, then normal issuance mechanics: deal value = stock FV (5,000 × $28 = 140,000, the determinable one) → land debited at it → par split as usual (50,000 par / 90,000 excess).

memorize.body: Noncash issuance: record at the more clearly determinable of (asset FV, stock FV). Actively traded stock almost always wins that contest.
If the stock does NOT trade actively (closely held company), the logic flips — the ASSET's appraised fair value measures the deal instead.
Never measure at par, at book value, or at the seller's asking price — three tempting numbers, all wrong.

questions.prompt: Land is acquired for 5,000 shares of $10-par stock trading at $28. Land is recorded at…

distractors.feedback: That's par × shares — legal capital, not value. Par measures nothing about the deal; the $28 market price does. Land = 5,000 × $28 = $140,000.

accountBank.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common

build.scaffold: Find the deal's ONE fair value first (the determinable one — here, the traded stock). Debit the asset at it, then run standard issuance mechanics: par to the stock account, excess to PIC.
